**Long Island University** (**LIU**) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") and [Brookville, New York](/wiki/Brookville,_New_York "Brookville, New York"), United States. LIU is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R2: Doctoral Universities – High research activity" according to the Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The university enrolls over 16,000 students and offers over 500 academic programs at its main campuses, [LIU Brooklyn](/wiki/LIU_Brooklyn "LIU Brooklyn") and [LIU Post](/wiki/LIU_Post "LIU Post") on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), in addition to non-residential locations and online. The [LIU Sharks](/wiki/LIU_Sharks "LIU Sharks") athletic teams compete in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") as a [Northeast Conference](/wiki/Northeast_Conference "Northeast Conference") member. LIU hosts and sponsors the annual [George Polk Awards](/wiki/George_Polk_Awards "George Polk Awards") in journalism.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/C.W._Post_College%2C_Brookville%2C_Long_Island._LOC_gsc.5a29903.jpg/250px-C.W._Post_College%2C_Brookville%2C_Long_Island._LOC_gsc.5a29903.jpg)](/wiki/File:C.W._Post_College,_Brookville,_Long_Island._LOC_gsc.5a29903.jpg)

C.W. Post College, Brookville, 1967

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 20th century")]

LIU was chartered by the [New York State Education Department](/wiki/New_York_State_Education_Department "New York State Education Department") in 1926 in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), to provide "effective and moderately priced education" to people from "all walks of life".[[5]](#cite_note-5) [LIU Brooklyn](/wiki/LIU_Brooklyn "LIU Brooklyn") is located in Downtown Brooklyn, at the corner of Flatbush and DeKalb Avenues. The main building adjoins the 1920s movie house [Paramount Theatre](/wiki/Paramount_Theatre_(Brooklyn) "Paramount Theatre (Brooklyn)"); the building retains much of the original decorative detail and a fully operational [Wurlitzer organ](/wiki/Wurlitzer_organ "Wurlitzer organ"). The campus consists of nine academic buildings; a recreation and athletic complex that includes Division I regulation athletic fields; one on-campus and two nearby residential buildings; and an adjoining parking facility.

The campus is home to the university's oldest school, the Arnold & Marie Schwartz College of Pharmacy and Health Sciences, founded in 1891 as the Brooklyn College of Pharmacy,[[6]](#cite_note-6) and [LIU Global](/wiki/LIU_Global "LIU Global"), a four-year bachelor's degree program that allows students to live and study internationally in eight countries across eight semesters.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The LIU athletic teams, the Sharks, compete at the [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") level[[8]](#cite_note-8) The university sponsors the [George Polk Awards](/wiki/George_Polk_Awards "George Polk Awards") for excellence in journalism,[[9]](#cite_note-9) and hosts and manages the Kumble Theater for the Performing Arts.

In 1951, in response to a growing number of families moving to the suburbs, LIU purchased an 177-acre (72 ha) estate known as **Hillwood** from cereal heiress [Marjorie Merriweather Post](/wiki/Marjorie_Merriweather_Post "Marjorie Merriweather Post") and her third husband Joseph E. Davies.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Located in [Brookville](/wiki/Brookville,_New_York "Brookville, New York") on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island")'s [Gold Coast](/wiki/North_Shore_(Long_Island) "North Shore (Long Island)"), the original home, Warburton Hall, was built by William A. Prime and was extensively renovated by Marjorie and her second husband [Edward F. Hutton](/wiki/Edward_F._Hutton "Edward F. Hutton"). Three years later, the campus was renamed C. W. Post, in honor of Marjorie Post's father [C. W. Post](/wiki/C._W._Post "C. W. Post"), a pioneering food entrepreneur.

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In 2012, the university renamed all campuses. C. W. Post is now [LIU Post](/wiki/LIU_Post "LIU Post"), the university's largest campus, at 307 acres (125 hectares) of historic 1920s mansions, gardens, athletic fields, art studios and performing arts space, broadcast television and radio stations, an on-campus [sustainable](/wiki/Sustainable "Sustainable") energy facility, and the only on-campus [equestrian facility](/wiki/Equestrian_facility "Equestrian facility") on Long Island. LIU Post was home to the [NCAA Division II](/wiki/NCAA_Division_II "NCAA Division II") [LIU Post Pioneers](/wiki/LIU_Post_Pioneers "LIU Post Pioneers") and is the site of the Tilles Center for the Performing Arts. The school introduced its first online degree plan in 2004.

In March 2013, LIU named Kimberly R. Cline the university's tenth president. She is the first woman to lead the private, six-campus institution.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

#### 2016–17 lockout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2016–17 lockout")]

Cline outsourced the work of two groups of previously [unionized](/wiki/Unionized "Unionized") workers on campus, and oversaw the [lockout](/wiki/Lockout_(industry) "Lockout (industry)") of 400 faculty on the day prior to the beginning of the 2016–17 school year.[[12]](#cite_note-thenation.com-12)

On September 1, 2016, three days after the union's contract expired and five days before the union was due to vote on the new contract,[[13]](#cite_note-13) the university cut off the affected staff's email accounts and health insurance, and told them they would be replaced.[[14]](#cite_note-14) This was the first time that a college or university in the United States has used a lockout against its faculty members, according to William A. Herbert, executive director of the [National Center for the Study of Collective Bargaining in Higher Education and the Professions](/wiki/National_Center_for_the_Study_of_Collective_Bargaining_in_Higher_Education_and_the_Professions "National Center for the Study of Collective Bargaining in Higher Education and the Professions").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Following the lockout, the [American Association of University Professors](/wiki/American_Association_of_University_Professors "American Association of University Professors") released a statement that it "deplores this action and supports the right of the LIU Brooklyn faculty to collectively bargain in good faith with its administration", and urged the LIU administration to resume negotiations.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In the first week of the autumn term, some students at LIU Brooklyn staged a [walkout](/wiki/Walkout "Walkout") in support of the locked-out teaching staff.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

With the 236 full-time faculty members and 450 adjuncts locked out, classes were taught by university administrators and temporary staff, and students reported inadequate instruction.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The lockout ended on September 14 with an agreement to continue the expired contract until May 31, 2017, and resume negotiations with a [mediator](/wiki/Mediation "Mediation").[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

#### COVID-19 pandemic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_University&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: COVID-19 pandemic")]

In response to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_(state) "COVID-19 pandemic in New York (state)"), Long Island University moved all classes to online instruction for the remainder of the Spring 2020 semester.[[21]](#cite_note-:0-21) Following a [stay-at-home order](/wiki/Stay-at-home_order "Stay-at-home order") from then-Governor [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo") directing all non-essential businesses to work remotely, administrative and academic offices began operating virtually and LIU fired or furloughed employees whose work was perceived as non-amenable to working remotely, including 84 of 98 unionized employees.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[21]](#cite_note-:0-21)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Instruction in summer 2020 was conducted online and LIU began offering in-person instruction again beginning on September 8, 2020, with online options for people unable to attend lectures.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Following the Thanksgiving recess, all instruction became online, with LIU resuming in-person instruction starting on February 1, 2021, at the start of the spring semester.

## Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Organization")]

LIU is administered by a president and a 27-member[[25]](#cite_note-25) board of trustees who elect the president.

### Lewyt College of Veterinary Medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_University&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Lewyt College of Veterinary Medicine")]

The veterinary college's inaugural class began instruction in Fall 2020. At the time of its founding, there were only 30 vet colleges in the United States.[[26]](#cite_note-cuomo-26) For over 150 years, the only vet school in the state was the [New York State College of Veterinary Medicine at Cornell University](/wiki/New_York_State_College_of_Veterinary_Medicine_at_Cornell_University "New York State College of Veterinary Medicine at Cornell University") in Ithaca, New York.[[27]](#cite_note-27) However, political pressure grew for a second school in the New York City area. In May 2018, New York State granted $12 million to LIU to develop a vet school.[[26]](#cite_note-cuomo-26) Pre-clinical instruction is based in Brentwood, NY. Instead of developing its own veterinary hospital, LIU's clinical programs are taught at existing veterinary hospitals and practices.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The LIU Vet School has received a provisional accreditation and awarded its first DVM degrees in 2024.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Other LIU locations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_University&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Other LIU locations")]

**LIU Brentwood** offers undergraduate and/or graduate programs in education, special education, literacy, mental health counseling, school counseling, psychology, criminal justice, and nursing.

**LIU Hudson** offers graduate and advanced certificate programs in business, public administration, pharmaceutics, education (early childhood, childhood, literacy, special education, and TESOL), educational leadership, school counseling, school psychology, mental health counseling, and marriage and family therapy.

**LIU Riverhead** is home to the Homeland Security Management Institute, which offers [homeland security](/wiki/Homeland_security "Homeland security") training. The institute has been designated a "Homeland Security Center of Excellence" by the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"). Programs are also available in education, special education, literacy, communication studies, new media, cyber security, applied behavior analysis, and [TESOL](/wiki/TESOL_International_Association "TESOL International Association").

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Long_Island_University&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [LIU Sharks](/wiki/LIU_Sharks "LIU Sharks")

On October 3, 2018, Long Island University announced that it was unifying the athletic programs of its two campuses into one Division I program, effective with the 2019–20 academic year.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The new program's nickname of Sharks was announced on May 15, 2019.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The Sharks retain the Brooklyn campus's affiliation in the [Northeast Conference](/wiki/Northeast_Conference "Northeast Conference").

The Sharks added two completely new women's sports effective in 2019–20. Shortly before the athletic merger was announced, LIU Brooklyn announced that it would add women's ice hockey; that sport will carry over to the unified program.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Shortly after the merger announcement, LIU announced it would add women's water polo, placing that sport in the [Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference](/wiki/Metro_Atlantic_Athletic_Conference "Metro Atlantic Athletic Conference").[[34]](#cite_note-34)
